perturbation.original_text: TPC agrees to provide Consolidated Artists and its representative, Advantage International Management, Inc. ("Advantage"), with a copy of all advertising and promotional materials which will use or show the Ogle Endorsement for their approval. Such promotional or advertising materials shall be delivered to Advantage at least fourteen (14) days prior to their release to the general public, and TPC agrees that the same shall not be released without the - 6 - prior written approval of Advantage. Advantage agrees that it will not unreasonably disapprove or reject promotional or advertising materials hereunder, and that Advantage's failure to disapprove such materials within fourteen (14) days of receipt shall be deemed to be approval. In addition, TPC agrees to provide Consolidated Artists with complimentary duplicates of all promotional and/or advertising materials featuring Ogle or the Ogle Endorsement.
perturbation.changed_text: TPC will endeavor to provide Consolidated Artists and its representative, Advantage International Management, Inc. ("Advantage"), with a copy of advertising and promotional materials showing the Ogle Endorsement. Such materials should be delivered to Advantage sometime before their release to the public, and TPC will aim to obtain approval from Advantage. Advantage will consider approving or rejecting the materials, and a lack of response may imply approval. In addition, TPC will try to provide Consolidated Artists with duplicates of advertising materials featuring Ogle or the Ogle Endorsement.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses weaker, less definitive language ('endeavor,' 'should,' 'sometime,' 'aim,' 'may imply,' 'try') instead of 'agrees' and 'shall,' creating ambiguity about the obligation to provide materials for approval and obtain explicit approval before release. This shift from mandatory to discretionary action weakens Consolidated Artists' control over Ogle's endorsement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act, 15 U.S.C. § 1114, protects trademark owners (in this case, Consolidated Artists, representing Brett Ogle) from unauthorized use of their trademarks. The original agreement ensures control over the endorsement through a strict approval process. By weakening the obligation for TPC to obtain prior written approval and deliver materials in a timely manner, the modified text could lead to unauthorized use of the Ogle endorsement, thus infringing on trademark rights protected by the Lanham Act. The term 'endeavor' and 'aim' are not definitive.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation of TPC to obtain approval for advertising materials using the Ogle endorsement, which can lead to unauthorized use and trademark infringement. The original text used definitive language like 'agrees' and 'shall,' ensuring control over the endorsement. The changed text uses vague terms such as 'endeavor,' 'should,' 'sometime,' 'aim,' and 'may imply,' thus creating ambiguity. This directly contradicts the Lanham Act, specifically 15 U.S.C. § 1114, which protects trademark owners from unauthorized use of their trademarks. The scraped law snippet states that 'Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant… use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark... shall be liable in a civil action.' By making the approval process less stringent, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized use of the Ogle endorsement, conflicting with the Lanham Act's protection of trademark rights.

perturbation.original_text: All payments to be made to Consolidated Artists pursuant to the terms hereof shall be made by wire transfer in U.S. Dollars to the following account: Account Name: Advantage International Escrow Fund Account #: 20068160-13 ABA #: 054001547 Bank Name: Franklin National Bank of Washington Address: 1722 Eye Street Washington, D.C. 20006 U.S.A. Re: Consolidated Artists
perturbation.changed_text: Payments to Consolidated Artists should generally be made by wire transfer in U.S. Dollars to the following account: Account Name: Advantage International Escrow Fund Account #: 20068160-13 ABA #: 054001547 Bank Name: Franklin National Bank of Washington Address: 1722 Eye Street Washington, D.C. 20006 U.S.A. Re: Consolidated Artists, or to another account as the parties may informally agree upon.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be made' to 'should generally be made' and adding 'or to another account as the parties may informally agree upon,' the obligation to make payments to the specified account becomes ambiguous. This allows for undocumented or informal payment arrangements, potentially circumventing legal and accounting requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Electronic Fund Transfer Act, 15 U.S.C. § 1693
perturbation.law_explanation: The Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA), 15 U.S.C. § 1693, requires clear documentation and tracking of electronic fund transfers to protect consumers. Allowing for 'informal' payment arrangements undermines the EFTA's requirements for record-keeping, error resolution, and consumer protection. It creates potential for disputes and makes it difficult to verify compliance with the contract.

Congressional findings and declaration of purpose (a) Informed use of credit The Congress finds that economic stabilization would be enhanced and the competition among the various financial institutions and other firms engaged in the extension of consumer credit would be strengthened by the informed use of credit. The informed use of credit results from an awareness of the cost thereof by consumers. It is the purpose of this subchapter to assure a meaningful disclosure of credit terms so that the consumer will be able to compare more readily the various credit terms available to him and avoid the uninformed use of credit, and to protect the consumer against inaccurate and unfair credit billing and credit card practices. (b) Terms of personal property leases The Congress also finds that there has been a recent trend toward leasing automobiles and other durable goods for consumer use as an alternative to installment credit sales and that these leases have been offered without adequate cost disclosures. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'shall be made' to 'should generally be made' and adds the possibility of 'informal' payment arrangements. This contradicts the Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA), 15 U.S.C. § 1693, as explained in the Law Explanation, which requires clear documentation and tracking of electronic fund transfers. Scraped Law Snippet 2 contains the language 'SUBCHAPTER VI—ELECTRONIC FUND TRANSFERS 1693. Congressional findings and declaration of purpose. 1693a. Definitions. 1693b. Regulations. 1693c. Terms and conditions of transfers. 1693d. Documentation of transfers' illustrating the need for documentation. Allowing informal payment methods undermines these documentation requirements, making compliance and verification difficult, thus creating a legal contradiction because it generates ambiguity and potential non-compliance with record-keeping regulations as the law requires documentation of transfers.

perturbation.original_text: TPC agrees that it shall keep accurate and complete books and records showing all Endorsed Products manufactured, distributed and sold. Consolidated Artists and Advantage, at Consolidated Artists' expense, shall have the right during the Contract Period and until two (2) years after the termination of this Agreement to inspect and make copies of the books and records of TPC insofar as they relate to the computation of royalty payments due and owing to Consolidated Artists hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: TPC will make an effort to maintain books and records relating to Endorsed Products. Consolidated Artists and Advantage may request to view records related to royalty payments, and TPC will consider such requests.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'TPC agrees that it shall keep accurate and complete books and records' to 'TPC will make an effort to maintain books and records' and 'shall have the right...to inspect and make copies' to 'may request to view records' weakens the obligation for accurate record-keeping and inspection rights. It introduces uncertainty about the standard of record-keeping and makes the inspection process discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Code § 8.2-707 - Goods sold on approval and sale or return; rights of creditors
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this section talks about goods and rights of creditors, its relevance is the implied duty of good faith under Virginia law, as emphasized by the UCC. By creating vague record-keeping responsibilities, the modified text allows TPC to potentially manipulate sales figures, reducing the royalty payments owed. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required in all contracts under Virginia law, particularly in contracts involving ongoing financial obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 8.2-707. "Person in the position of a seller." LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 8.2. Commercial Code - Sales » Part 7. Remedies » § 8.2-707. "Person in the position of a seller." Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 8.2. Commercial Code - Sales Part 7. Remedies 6/15/2025 § 8.2-707 . "Person in the position of a seller." (1) A "person in the position of a seller" includes as against a principal an agent who has paid or become responsible for the price of goods on behalf of his principal or anyone who otherwise holds a security interest or other right in goods similar to that of a seller. (2) A person in the position of a seller may as provided in this title withhold or stop delivery (§ 8.2-705 ) and resell (§ 8.2-706 ) and recover incidental damages (§ 8.2-710 ). 1964, c. 219. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens TPC's obligation to maintain accurate records and alters Consolidated Artists' right to inspect those records. The original text states TPC 'shall keep accurate and complete books and records,' and Consolidated Artists 'shall have the right...to inspect and make copies.' The changed text softens this to 'TPC will make an effort to maintain books and records' and 'may request to view records,' which introduces ambiguity. Although the law snippet discusses the rights of a seller, the LLM's Law Explanation argues that this change violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Virginia law, particularly concerning ongoing financial obligations and royalty payments.
